_sources.discrepancies: NCSL lists MS as 'strict photo ID' — one of the strictest categories. SoS site confirms voters without photo ID must vote a provisional ballot and present ID before a set deadline or it will not be counted. Consistent.

earlyVoting.notes: Mississippi does not have early voting. Absentee voting is available for qualifying voters.
No early voting for runoffs. TODO verify runoff date for 2026 cycle.
Mississippi does not have early voting. Absentee voting is available for qualifying voters.
Mississippi does not have a general early voting period. Absentee voting is available for voters who qualify (illness, disability, absence, etc.).

acceptedIds: Mississippi driver's license or state ID card
Mississippi voter ID card (free from circuit clerk)
votingRules.expirationRule: Photo ID must not be expired except for Mississippi driver's licenses which may be used if expired within 10 years. TODO verify for 2026 cycle.
votingRules.impedimentDeclaration: Voters without photo ID must vote provisionally and present a valid photo ID to the circuit clerk's office by noon 5 business days after the election. Alternatively, they may cast a provisional ballot and sign an affidavit at a circuit court within that window.
votingRules.phonesAtPollsDetail: Mississippi prohibits use of mobile phones and other communication devices in the polling booth.
additionalRules: Open primary: any registered voter may vote in any party's primary.
Runoff rule: if no candidate receives a majority in the primary, the top two advance to a runoff. A voter who voted in one party's primary may only vote in that party's runoff.

eligibility: Will be absent from the county on Election Day
Religious objection to Saturday voting (for Saturday special elections)
voteByMail.applicationDeadline: Must be received by the circuit clerk 5 days before the election
voteByMail.returnDeadline: Must be received by the circuit clerk by 5 p.m. on Election Day
voteByMail.returnDeadlinePlain: Absentee ballot must arrive at the circuit clerk by 5 p.m. on Election Day. Postmarks do not count.

runoffRules.ruleExplanation: Mississippi rule: if you voted in one party's primary, you can only vote in that same party's runoff. If you did not vote in the primary, you may choose either party's runoff.
